food_subgroup: Crustaceans — cooked crab dish with salted egg sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for salted egg crab. FOODON_03302757 (crab food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Crabs, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for salted egg crab preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226808007 represents crab as a substance; no specific concept for salted egg crab dish exists.

main_ingredients: whole crab (mud crab or Sri Lanka crab)

serving.description: 1 whole crab (~500 g edible portion including shell)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of salted egg crab is one whole crab weighing approximately 500 grams (including shell), yielding roughly 150–200 g of edible crab meat coated in salted egg sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Crab, cooked — nutrient values adapted with salted egg sauce composition; primary crab data from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 15139) and salted egg yolk sauce component estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD and USDA Branded/SR Legacy ingredient data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg crab as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining: (1) cooked crab meat per 100 g from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 174202, Blue crab cooked) as the protein/mineral base; (2) salted egg yolk sauce contribution (butter, salted duck egg yolk, evaporated milk, curry leaves, chilli) estimated from USDA SR Legacy and IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data. The dish is approximately 40% crab meat and 60% sauce by edible weight. Values represent the composite edible portion per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — shellfish and protein-dominant seafood dishes; analogous estimate based on cooked crab and egg-based sauce with negligible carbohydrate content
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for salted egg crab. GI is estimated at 5 based on the University of Sydney GI Database classification of protein- and fat-dominant seafood dishes with very low carbohydrate content (carbohydrate_g ~4.2 per 100 g). Shellfish and egg-based dishes with minimal starch consistently fall in the GI 1–10 range. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (5 × 21 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 1.05, rounded to 0.4 per 100 g portion basis. GL per serving (150 g edible) ≈ (5 × 6.3) / 100 = 0.3, reported as 0.4 conservatively.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~620 mg per 100 g) from salted duck egg yolk and added salt in sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit salted egg crab to occasional consumption and request reduced-salt preparation, as a single restaurant serving (500 g whole crab, ~150 g edible) may contribute approximately 930 mg sodium, nearly 40% of the 2,400 mg daily limit recommended by the Singapore Health Promotion Board.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~4.2 g per 100 g) and low glycemic index (~5) with minimal glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Salted egg crab has a negligible glycemic load due to its very low carbohydrate content, making it a suitable protein-rich choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat from butter and egg yolk sauce warrants moderation to support cardiovascular risk management in this population.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat (~6.5 g per 100 g) and cholesterol (~145 mg per 100 g) from salted egg yolk and butter sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The salted egg yolk and butter sauce contribute significant saturated fat and dietary cholesterol; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency, as a 150 g edible serving provides approximately 9.8 g saturated fat, approaching the 10 g daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association for high-risk individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Salted Egg Crab (咸蛋螃蟹; Ketam Telur Masin) is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean tze char dish featuring whole live crab — typically mud crab (Scylla serrata) or Sri Lanka crab — that is cleaned, chopped, and wok-fried in a rich, creamy salted duck egg yolk sauce. The sauce is made by mashing cured salted duck egg yolks and combining them with butter, evaporated milk, curry leaves, dried chilli, garlic, and a touch of sugar, producing a golden, foamy, intensely savoury coating that clings to the crab shell and meat. The texture of the dish is a contrast of firm, sweet crab meat and a luscious, slightly grainy egg yolk sauce with aromatic herbal notes from curry leaves. Salted egg crab is a premium restaurant dish, typically ordered for sharing at Chinese seafood restaurants and tze char stalls across Singapore and Malaysia, and is priced at a premium due to the cost of live crab. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein from crab meat (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g edible portion), rich in omega-3 fatty acids, zinc, selenium, and vitamin B12, but also elevated in sodium (approximately 620 mg per 100 g) and saturated fat (approximately 6.5 g per 100 g) from the egg yolk and butter sauce. The glycemic index is very low (estimated GI ~5) due to minimal carbohydrate content, making it suitable for blood glucose management, though the sodium and saturated fat content require moderation for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for those with crustacean, egg, or dairy allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for salted egg crab as a composite prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central cooked crab data (FDC ID 174202) with salted egg yolk sauce component estimates from USDA SR Legacy and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. The estimation methodology is transparent and defensible, but the composite nature of the dish and restaurant-level variability in sauce richness introduce uncertainty. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database principles for low-carbohydrate seafood dishes. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source database for this composite dish; crab meat contains trace vitamin D but the value is not reliably quantified across the sauce composite. 2. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and trans fat values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy butter and crab meat component data; trans fat is reported as 0.1 g from butter contribution. 3. Halal status is marked false because salted egg crab as served at tze char restaurants (including the source restaurant George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer) is typically not halal-certified; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 4. The serving size of 500 g refers to the whole crab including shell; edible yield is approximately 150–200 g. 5. GI is estimated at 5 (effectively very low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous classification for protein- and fat-dominant seafood dishes with minimal starch; gi_category is low per the ≤55 threshold rule. 6. Nutrient values represent the composite edible portion (crab meat plus sauce) per 100 g, not the whole crab with shell. 7. The food_name has been normalised from the input menu name 'Salted Egg Crab x2 (~500grams x 2)' to the canonical English dish name 'Salted Egg Crab'; the quantity and weight descriptor are captured in the serving section.
